speaker: interviewer
text: --Finkel. This is tape number 6. Mr. Finkel, let's go back to 1951. You came to America. What did you do when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when I came here, I had a job with a department store called Mendel Brothers-- it's defunct. I was a salesman in the men's-- men's department, boys wear department.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, your English was-- was fairly fluent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was very good, probably better than it is now. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --schooling in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long were you there for? At Mendel Brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there, I think, about a year, or so, and a half. And I got sick with tuberculosis. I was-- I was reporting for my-- for my induction to the armed forces, and they found out I had some tuberculosis on my lungs. So I went to Winfield, and I spent like nine months there getting well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did you do when you got out of Winfield?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got out of Winfield-- I met somebody at the Winfield Sanatorium that was a-- an executive at Polk Brothers, which was, at that time, starting up as a big chain of appliance and furniture stores. And they got me a job there. And I worked there for almost 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, 40 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boy. What did-- what did you do there during the 40 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically I was a salesman, and I-- I managed departments. I was-- I managed a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was still about what? 1992, or so?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1992. April of 1992 is when they decided to go out of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Polk had died, and the family didn't want to continue anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do? At-- at that point, I had already kind of separate-- begin to separate myself from Polk Brothers, in the sense that I was thinking about, you know, retirement. And I was able to get into where I was buying properties, and rehabbing them, and selling them from the company. Wherever there was-- we could buy hot properties, we did, and we sold them. And we-- you know, my wife and I worked at it. And we probably did about-- we didn't do a lot, maybe about 10-- 10 projects, or so. And then, a year-- like, a year and a half after that, I was-- and I wasn't employed, but my health care insurance was running out, this COBRA that Polk gave me for 18 months. And we were scared. Still are about health coverage, although, thank God, we've never been sick. I went back to work in-- in Fretter's, which is an appliance chain. And that's now called Silo, but it's the same company.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very happy to get me. And I worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I arranged with them, I only work four days, seven hours, days. So I'm basically-- I'm now 63, I'm getting a pension from Polk Brothers. I have retired-- I-- I have Social Security, and I get a pension from Germany. So it's adequate to keep me in, what I would call, an OK style-- not extravagant, by any means, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were-- when did-- who is-- what was the name of your first wife? What is the name of your first wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first wife's name is Eve. She was-- her maiden name was-- Eve-- no, Eve Noparstak.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could You spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-O-P-A-R-S-T-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married her, I think, in 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed marriage for about five or six years. We had two children, Leon and Ruth. And my first wife was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. How old is Leon today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't he? Hm? More? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How old's Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Ruth is 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What does Leon do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon is a lawyer. [INAUDIBLE] Do I need to mention the law name's firm? [LAUGHING] I can't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he's a-- a-- a family lawyer? Matrimonial lawyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a matrimonial, divorce lawyer, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth works for Domino's Pizza. She's been transferred to the corporate headquarters, in Michigan. And she's an executive trainer. She trains-- so I think she has the Midwest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then, you-- you married your present wife, Jean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Jean and I have been married 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anderson. She's of Swedish descent.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you have one-- one-- one child with Jean, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one child-- we have one child with Jean. And that's-- the name is Lisa. She's 25 years old now. But when I married Jean, she had Kim, who, at that time, was like six years old, and Jackie, who was about three years old. And I consider them like my own children.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what do Kim, Jackie, and Lisa do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lisa works for Kmart. She works in the automobile department. She's a ticket writer, service writer. Jackie-- Jackie works for Cook Travel Agency. She's-- she has like 40 or 50 people working under her. She's an executive there. Kim is the executive assistant to Flossmoor police chief.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What does-- what does your wife, Jean, do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Jean, works part-- three days a week as a merchandiser for Warner-Lambert. That's the chewing gum people, and medication, and all kinds of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything else? You want to just talk about your family? Your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We're very happy with our grandchildren. We have-- the oldest one is Elizabeth. She's 11 years old. And she was the firstborn. And she was-- that's Kim's daughter. Next one to have been born was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hm, was-- was her brother, Eric. I think he's 10 or 8. And the-- Leon's two children, which is Rebecca and Barry. Jackie's little girl, Kirsten, and-- and Ruthie's little boy is one year old. And his name is Isaac. He's called Ike. He was named after my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Isaac died when? In 1992.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lola's still alive in Tucson?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you'd like to say about any members of your family, while you have the opportunity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- yeah, I-- I think so. I consider myself very fortunate that the kids are really very, very nice. I think this-- it hasn't always been like that, but I-- I think I've worked very hard to establish a relationship. It's been stormy at times, but I think, at this point, I think it's very, very good. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you've just opened up and-- and told this Holocaust story, just in the past few years. Has-- has-- has your-- your family relationships, relationship with your wife and your children, changed since then? Has it gotten better?

speaker: interviewee
text: My relationship with-- with my kids has gotten better, I think so, as a result of the work that I do. And the work I'm referring to is that I, now, for a year, have been a speaker for the Holocaust Foundation in Skokie. And I've spoken at about 10 different schools, so far. And I have, like, three or four other dates waiting. And I go to schools, and I talk to them, the kids. And apparently, something, almost what I would call magical, has happened, that I was-- I have been able to, because of who I am and my experiences, of really tell my story in such a way that the kids are affected by it. They're interested in it. They write me wonderful letters. And I love young people. And I'm very, very grateful that I have this opportunity of doing that. And I'm looking forward-- next Thursday I'll be speaking in a school with 400 children, which is the most I've ever would have spoken to. And I-- I-- basically, I just tell them my story. I don't lecture them. And I try to tell them the story from the eight-year-old point of view, so they can associate with it and go with my feelings. And I've had wonderful, wonderful-- this late in life, this has happened to me. And I feel this is like a-- something from something higher, because I never aimed to do it. I never planned to do it. It's like something more powerful and higher than me has directed me to do this work. And I-- I think it's very gratifying, although it's emotionally straining. I think I'm glad that I will be doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Look-- looking back at-- at all those terrible years in-- in-- in the war, could you define what would possibly be the worst-- your-- your worst moment, where you felt the lowest possible you-- you could feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the one I've described to you, the-- the-- the train, being thrown into that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Bugaj?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, at-- being thrown into that train, I think, was the worst. Yeah, that was like total disillusionment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was the happiest moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the war? Seeing my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seeing your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the resistance, yeah. I would say that was the only happy moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I guess, at this point, I want to thank you, very much, for coming here and telling us your story. And it will go on for generations. And thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: [TEARFUL] I want to thank everybody, the cameramen, all the people that are doing this work. This-- I think it's fantastic. And the people who are sponsoring it, Spielberg. I think it's a wonderful way to put one's assets to work. Like I told you, other people have money and assets, but they don't do this type of work. And I think it's very meaningful to them. I'm grateful to the foundation for this opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any closing thoughts on-- you know, any lessons that are-- that can be learned from this horrible tragedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think what-- what-- what is happening, from my experience at speaking in the schools, is that this is no longer about Jews' Holocaust. It has taken on the function of the-- this is the biggest one, the biggest picture, and we should-- that we include all the atrocities that human beings do to each other. This is like an umbrella for all the atrocities that go on today and have been going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we explain the Holocaust, we're really explaining the terrible human nature of other human beings killing other human beings. Because the minute that we allow ourself to think that somebody is different-- because their skin is different, their clothes are different-- then we become dehumanized. And we say it's OK to do that to those people, because we don't see them as human beings anymore. I think this is the big lesson. I think so. OK. Thank you very much, Mr. Finkel.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Mm-hm. This is my sister, Ronia, my oldest-- the oldest one in our family. She probably was about 25 years old, 27 maybe, at that time. Probably around 1937, '38. OK, this is the earliest picture that I have of me, of my life, basically. This was taken in 1945. This is our-- our arrival in England. And I'm in the middle, handing over a little boy to my brother, who is-- his hand's out there to reach him. And the two boys on the side, I don't know who they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you get that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- that was in-- I got that-- it was published in a British newspaper. And it's-- it's in-- in the Journal of the '45 Aid Society, which is an organization of the boys that came over to England from Theresienstadt. That's fine. This is a picture of-- I think it was taken in England. Of course, I know it was taken in England. And I think it was in Belfast. This is-- was a place where the Jews Refugee Committee-- it was like a hostel that the young people stayed. And these are the people that came over with-- from Theresienstadt to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, although I was close to a couple of them, but I don't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture shows me smoking a-- a cigar. I'm at a wedding in London. I think the lady's name was [? Helfgott-- ?] Ben's sister. I can't remember her name. And that's my brother, Isaac, and my sister-in-law, Gerty, who I'm going to be seeing in-- in a few weeks. She's coming to Israel with us. This is my cousin, Anna, the older one, with her daughter, Rita. Anna was with me in Bugaj. She-- through some miraculous event, she was able to survive with her two daughters, who were considerably younger than-- than I am. And-- but she suffered a lot. She never was able to mentally recover from her ordeal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camps was she in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen. She was with me in Piotrkow. She is the-- she is the daughter of my Uncle Leopold, my-- my-- my father's brother, my little brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were her two daughters with her the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole time. And like Rita was probably born in-- around '34. And she has a younger sister, Ellen, who is probably about four years younger than that. So it was just-- she has to be-- especially her sister, Ellen, who doesn't have much memory of what happened-- she's probably one of the very youngest that actually has gone through a concentration camp. Others have been hidden and so forth, but she has actually gone through a concentration camp. So it's-- she's quite something. She-- she also gave her testimony here. This is a picture of me. The year is probably 1952. I'm at Winfield Sanitarium. That's when I was-- when I had my tuberculosis. And that's in the hospital room. Oh, see. This is my first wife, Eve Noparstak. She is the mother of Leon and Ruth. She did a good job of bringing them up, for which I'm grateful. And this is the fruit of my marriage to Eve. This is Leon and Ruth, when they were very little. This is a-- a early picture of my sister, Lola, and her son, Luke. My sister, Lola, is alive, and she lives in Arizona. Luke, because he was born in Germany after the war, and she was taking some kind of medication, he is mentally retarded. And he is in a little Palatine city-- a-- a little city in Palatine. I see- I go to see him one-- once a month, or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I believe, was taken in-- the-- I think it was taken in Chicago. This is Eugene Talbert. He's the husband of my sister, Lola. He was also a survivor. He had been married previously, and lost his wife and child in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened to Lola? Where was she? What camps was she in?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I'm not sure. I can't answer that, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about her husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know either. At that time, I really wasn't involved in any of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he deceased now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is-- yeah, he's been dead about eight, nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very recent picture, 1994. And this is my lovely wife, Jean, to whom I've been married 30 years. And we are in Leon's house, sitting on their white sofa. This is a picture of my extended family. In this picture, the one in the white dress, next to my wife, with a flower, is Eve, my first wife. In the very corner, the blonde, is Jackie. She's my daughter. And the bride is Kim. She's my daughter. And in the background, the tall person, is my first wife's husband, Ron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon's back there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Leon is back there too. Right. Leon is back there too. Her-- his first girlfriend, Susan, I think it was. And the-- the-- the person that Kim married was-- his name was Andrew [? Wagaman. ?] He's of German descent. His father was in the German Army during the war. That's all. I love this picture. Here, this is-- I'm sure it's at Christmas time with some-- my grandchildren. The oldest one holding the camcorder-- oh, and Eric-- is Elizabeth. There's Eric and Rebecca. And I'm reading them a story from And we have-- this is in our house, a couple of years back. All right. OK, this is the latest addition to our family. This is Ike. Isaac is his name. It's Ruthie's little boy. And he's just about a year old here. And he's-- he's just a wonderful little kid. I'm going to be seeing him for Passover, in about a week. This is Kirsten. This is Jackie's and Marty's little girl. And she's a real sweetheart. And she's just waiting impatiently because, in another few weeks, she's going to have a little brother. So she's really excited. She's a real joy to behold. Main reason we're showing this picture is to show my best-loved daughter-in-law, Laura, who's a real sweetheart. We're-- I'm very, very close to her. This is at her wedding to Leon. I love her very, very much. This is the extended grandchildren, who are my joy. And this is-- everybody's in this picture, including Barry. This is the first picture of Barry. And the only one that's missing is Ike, but I don't think he was born then. Oh, he wasn't at my house, anyway, when this picture was taken. So this is the five grandchildren. This is Leon's family. There's Joey, Barry, and Rebecca, and Laura, and of course, Leon with the glasses. That's a very nice family, indeed. Joey is Laura's son from her first marriage, but he's a wonderful, wonderful young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Finkel, I know you-- you're very proud, and you should be, of-- of speaking to the kids at school. And you've gotten several letters from schools thanking you for speaking, and from students. And this is one that you received very recently, on April 1 of 1995, from Phillip Conrad, who was at the Francis Parker school. I know you're-- you know, this-- this letter meant something to you when you read it. Why don't you read that for the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just so we can show an example of what these kids--

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Mr. Finkel, I'm one of the privileged people who got to hear your story last Thursday at Francis Parker school. You probably don't know who I am. I was the kid who asked you whom you thought was responsible for the Holocaust. Anyways, I wish to thank you for coming to talk to us. It was an experience I will never forget. I remember seeing films and reading and then putting these pictures to your words. Your talk was especially powerful to me, because I'm a German. I came to the United States in '86, when I was six years old. Until Thursday, I had never had a full understanding of the Holocaust. That night, I spoke to my father, who was born a few years after the war. And we had a long conversation about what you said and how Germany was while he was growing up during and the aftermath of the war. He explained that Germany was so shocked about what had happened, the Germans that didn't know what was happening to the Jews, that teachers were too scared to teach their children about the Holocaust in school. I volunteered to send people the questions to you via email. I want to thank you, again greatly, for speaking to us. I will never forget it. Sincerely, Phillip Conrad.'

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, Mr Finkel.
